text.fullText: From: Betsy Cornwell <noreply@myfor.ms> To: OW-Docket <OW-Docket@epa.gov> Date: 2/4/2026 12:07:10 AM Subject: Docket ID No. EPA-HQ-OW-2025-2929 ------------------------------------------ Caution: This email originated from outside EPA, please exercise additional caution when deciding whether to open attachments or click on provided links. I am a US citizen, resident in Canada and I have a very fixed and vehement opinion about the health of the Great Lakeds I strongly oppose the EPA’s proposed revisions to the Clean Water Act Section 401 water quality certification regulations. The proposal would unlawfully and dangerously narrow the authority of states and tribes to protect their waters from harmful projects — especially large oil and gas pipelines. By limiting review to narrowly defined “discharges” and constraining timelines and conditions, the rule undermines Congress’s clear intent that states and tribes serve as frontline protectors of water quality. This rollback is especially concerning for the Great Lakes, which hold 20% of the world’s surface fresh water and supply drinking water to more than 40 million people. In Michigan,and at least an equal number of people on both side of the border along the St. Lawrence waterway. The proposal could directly affect decisions regarding Enbridge’s Line 5 oil pipeline, including a proposed tunnel and potential horizontal directional drilling beneath the Straits of Mackinac—an area of extraordinary ecological, cultural, and economic significance. In addition to the Enbridge problem the renewed proposal to mine copper in the Boundary Waters area, again a shared resource protected by both Canada and the US, is a land mine that greed from South American mining interests could explode. Enbridge has delayed a final decision on its Clean Water Act Section 404 permit from the Michigan Department of Environment, Great Lakes, and Energy. If EPA finalizes this rule, it could affect the outcome by restricting Michigan’s ability—and tribal nations’ treaty-protected authority—to fully evaluate and reject water quality risks. States and tribes must retain the power to: • Consider cumulative and long-term water quality impacts • Protect treaty rights, fisheries, and drinking water • Deny certification when projects threaten water quality standards The EPA must withdraw this proposal and instead strengthen, not weaken, Clean Water Act protections. The Great Lakes are too important to risk for the convenience of oil pipeline companies. It will not be long before US claims on the Great Lakes are made to supply its waters to the parched southwest quadrant of the US.which is likely to spark serious confrontations between the US and Canada. If the US contaminates those same waters before they can even attempt to purloin them just who is going to be held responsible? The EPA's failure of stewardship! Sincerely, Betsy Cornwell Erin, Ontario
